sections.title: Personality
sections.subtitle: Filter 1 of 5
sections.description: Discover your Personal Behavioural Design using the D.I.S.C. model. Understanding your personality preferences helps you work with your natural tendencies, improve relationships, and reduce stress in every area of life.

opening_quote.text: Until you make the unconscious conscious, it will direct your life and you will call it fate.

framing.opening_question: If we asked the five people closest to you to describe how you make decisions, would they all give the same answer?
framing.facilitator_says: D.I.S.C. isn't a label — it's a mirror. Today we're going to look at how you naturally show up, before we look at how you choose to show up.
framing.why_it_matters: Strategy starts with self-knowledge. You can't plan a life you don't understand. Your D.I.S.C. style shapes how you make decisions, who energises you, and what kind of work feels effortless. Knowing it doesn't lock you in — it gives you a starting point.
framing.closing_reflection: What is one D.I.S.C. trait that helps you, and one that gets in your way?
framing.bridge_to_next: Knowing your style is one filter. Now we look at the lenses you see through it: your attitudes.

reading_material.title: How To Read and Understand People — Bill Bonnstetter (TTI)
reading_material.content: A short primer on the D.I.S.C. behavioural model — the framework underneath this section. Bonnstetter explains why the four primary styles (Dominance, Influence, Steadiness, Compliance) are observable patterns, not fixed labels, and how each one tends to make decisions, communicate, and react under pressure. The PSP workshop adapts this work with permission.

exercises.title: D.I.S.C. — Discovering My Personal Behavioural Design
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.content:
The D.I.S.C. MODEL was developed by Bill Bonnstetter at Target Training International, Phoenix, Arizona. It is based on the psychology of Carl Jung and William Moulton Marston and is widely accepted as an effective tool for enhancing performance in all areas of life.

Through the combined Nature/Nurture process of growing, you have developed specific ways of interacting with the world. This is your Personal Behavioural Style, or CORE STYLE.

The D.I.S.C. model uses four factors to determine behaviour:

1. THE DOMINANCE FACTOR (D) — How you handle problems and challenges
2. THE INFLUENCE FACTOR (I) — How you handle people and influence others
3. THE STEADINESS FACTOR (S) — How you handle and pace yourself
4. THE COMPLIANCE FACTOR (C) — How you handle the rules set by others

How you will benefit:
• Increased Self Understanding
• Increased Understanding of Others
• Increased Communication Skills
• Less Stress
• Decreased Tension
• Improved Relationships (Family, Work/Business, Social)
content_json.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

exercises.title: EXERCISE: Identifying Your Own Personal Style
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.prompt: In the following four categories (D, I, S, C), check which of the statements you tend to agree with more. Rate how strongly (1 to 10) you agree with each statement. Your highest rating indicates your BASIC CORE STYLE.

options.label: D — Dominance: You tend to be very active and aggressive in gaining results. You will go directly at the problem with little or no fear. (How you handle problems and challenges)
I — Influence: You usually are very approachable and open to others. Outgoing, social, very verbally persuasive. (How you interact with other people)

S — Steadiness: You prefer a structured and more predictable environment, having the parameters and boundaries clearly defined. Prefer a secure situation. (How you handle a steady pace and your workplace)

C — Compliance: You tend to follow rules set by others and you are very aware of the consequences of not complying with rules or following procedures. (How you respond to rules and procedures imposed by others)

content_json.prompt:
Based on the D.I.S.C. exercise above, what is your Core Style? Use the two-question method to confirm: (1) Am I predominantly EXTROVERTED or INTROVERTED? (2) Am I predominantly PEOPLE-ORIENTED or TASK-ORIENTED?

Extroverted + Task-Oriented = Core D | Extroverted + People-Oriented = Core I | Introverted + People-Oriented = Core S | Introverted + Task-Oriented = Core C

Write your Core Style and reflect on how it shows up in your daily life:

sections.title: Attitudes
sections.subtitle: Filter 2 of 5
sections.description: Explore the six attitude types identified by Eduard Spranger and discover how your unique world view shapes your values, motivations, and the choices you make in life.

opening_quote.text: Between stimulus and response there is a space. In that space is our power to choose our response.

framing.opening_question: What is one belief you hold about yourself that you have never actually questioned?
framing.facilitator_says: Attitudes are the lenses we look through. We rarely notice them — but they shape every decision we make.
framing.why_it_matters: Even with the right personality and the right skills, the wrong attitude will sabotage you. The good news: attitudes can be examined, named, and chosen. We're going to surface yours so you can decide which to keep, which to soften, and which to retire.
framing.closing_reflection: Which one attitude — if you changed it tomorrow — would change the most about your life?
framing.bridge_to_next: Attitudes are HOW you see. Values are WHAT you protect. Let's go there next.

reading_material.title: Man's Search for Meaning — Viktor Frankl
reading_material.content: The book the opening quote comes from. Frankl, a psychiatrist who survived the Nazi camps, argues that meaning is not given to us — it is the response we choose to whatever we are handed. For this section, the relevant move is his: you cannot always change the stimulus, but the space between stimulus and response is where your attitude lives. Naming that space is the first step in this exercise.

exercises.title: What Is an Attitude?
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.content:
In 1928, Eduard Spranger wrote and published The Types of Men. Spranger observed that all people view the world differently. Whenever several people viewed the same object or event, they invariably placed different values upon it. Through his research, Spranger identified 6 world views that affect the values people place upon objects or events. He labelled these views as Attitudes.

An attitude is:
1. A world view — a way of looking at life
2. A paradigm of thought
3. A value or judgment of life
4. A mindset

Spranger noted that:
• VALUES AND ATTITUDES → create → WANTS
• WANTS → determine → BEHAVIOUR
• BEHAVIOUR → achieves → RESULTS

We all see the world through our own attitude-tinted eyes. And we either reject or value various aspects of life based upon our attitude towards them. According to Spranger, there is no such thing as a 'good' or 'bad' attitude.
content_json.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

exercises.title: EXERCISE: Identifying Your Attitudes
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.prompt:
From the following list of likes and dislikes, select those that most accurately describe who you are. The first answer that comes to your mind is usually a good indication of how you truly feel.

Group W (Theoretical — Questions 1–9):
Group A (Aesthetic — Questions 10–18):
Group T (Traditional — Questions 19–25):
Group U (Utilitarian — Questions 26–35):
Group S (Social — Questions 36–42):
Group I (Individualistic — Questions 43–50):

Check all that apply, then count your totals per group to find your top two attitudes.

options.label: I like solving problems.
I like objectivity in all areas of life.
I like identifying, differentiating, generalizing, systematizing.
I like intellectual process.
I like discovery, understanding, ordering.
I like pursuit of knowledge, identifying truth and untruth.
I like knowledge for the sake of knowing.
I hate inability to know, learn or discover.
I hate emotional, subjective experience without rational justification.
I like appreciation of form, harmony and beauty.
I like enjoyment of all the senses.
I like subjective experience.
I like understanding the feelings of myself and others.
I like self-realization, self-fulfilment and self-actualization.
I like creative expression.
I like appreciation of impressions.
I hate disturbances in form, beauty and harmony in myself, others and/or the environment.
I object to objective truth.
I like understanding the totality of life.
I like finding meaning in life.
I like pursuit of the divine in life.
I like following and dying for a cause.
I like living constantly according to a 'closed' book.
I like converting others to my way of thinking.
I don't like people who oppose my beliefs.
I like practicality in all areas of my life.
I like surpassing others in the attainment of wealth.
I like using resources to accomplish results.
I like gaining a measurable return on all my investments.
I like creative application of resources.
I like producing goods, materials, services and marketing them for economic gain.
I like capitalism.
I like maximizing or conserving resources.
I don't like to see wasted resources, time, materials and/or services.
I hate investments with inadequate or no return.
I like investing in others.
I like selflessness.
I like generosity of time, talent and resources.
I like seeing and developing potential in others.
I like championing a worthy cause.
I don't like rampant individualism.
I hate decisions and actions which are insensitive to people's needs.
I like leading others.
I like achieving position.
I like advancing position and strategic alliances.
I like attaining and using power to accomplish purpose.
I like planning and carrying out a winning strategy.
I like tactics and positioning.
I hate actual or perceived threat of diminished power or position.
I hate inability to advance, lack of opportunity for individual advancement.

content_json.prompt: Based on your checklist results, rank these six attitude types from highest (most checks) to lowest. Your top two attitudes reveal your primary world view and motivational drivers.

items.label: W — The Theoretical Attitude: I use my intellect to systematically understand and discover the truth. The discovery of Truth and Knowledge is my main motivation in life. I seek knowledge for its own sake.
A — The Aesthetic Attitude: I enjoy and experience the beauty around me and find meaning and fulfilment within the experience of living. My main motivation is to experience the Inner Vision.
T — The Traditional Attitude: I pursue and understand the highest meaning of life within an established system of beliefs. My main motivation is to search and find the highest value in life.
U — The Utilitarian Attitude: I see most things in terms of their usefulness and practicality. My main motivation is to turn what I do and have into a useful purpose.

S — The Social Attitude: I enjoy helping others and believe that all decisions should be sensitive to people's needs. My main goal in life is to do what I can to eliminate hate and conflict in the world.
I — The Individualistic Attitude: I want to achieve the highest position possible and wield the greatest power in my chosen field and will do everything within my ability to reach these goals.

content_json.prompt:
Reflect on the following Attitude Power Points and write how your top two attitudes show up in your daily life, career choices, and relationships:

1. An attitude is a way of valuing life — a world view, a paradigm of thought and action.
2. Most of a person's choices throughout life are guided by the hierarchy of their attitudes and needs satisfactions.
3. Attitudes determine our purpose and direction in life, stimulating us to action.
4. Attitudes are relatively constant throughout life and will usually change only in relation to a Significant Emotional Event.
5. Behaviour (D.I.S.C.) is the methodology for fulfilling the passions driven by our attitudes.
6. Attitudes tend to interact with one another.

How do your top two attitude types interact? Do they support or conflict with each other?
content_json.placeholder: My top two attitudes are ___ and ___. I notice them showing up in my life when...

sections.title: Values
sections.subtitle: Filter 3 of 5
sections.description: Uncover what truly matters to you through the Values Shopping Spree exercise. Your values form the background of your conscious and unconscious goals and propel your actions — whether you recognize it or not.

opening_quote.text: It's not hard to make decisions when you know what your values are.

framing.opening_question: If you had to give up everything you own except five things — and not material things, but five values — what would you protect?
framing.facilitator_says: Values aren't what we say we believe. They are what we spend on — time, energy, attention. Today we make that visible.
framing.why_it_matters: Your values are your compass. The Goal Setting section later in this course only works if you have named which direction is north. Most people inherit their values from family, school, or workplace and never audit them. The Values Shopping Spree exercise is your audit.
framing.closing_reflection: Which of your top five values is currently underfed?
framing.bridge_to_next: You know your style, your lenses, your compass. Now: what hats are you actually wearing day-to-day?

reading_material.title: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People — Stephen R. Covey
reading_material.content: Covey's framework treats values as the centre of a personal mission and warns about the cost of confusing values with urgencies. Habit 2 — Begin with the end in mind — is the lens to bring into the Shopping Spree exercise: you are not buying what feels nice today, you are buying the things that, ten years from now, you would still defend as worth the money. Covey also separates principles (universal) from values (chosen) — useful vocabulary if you find the exercise hard.

content_json.prompt:
CONGRATULATIONS! You have won an imaginary $100,000! But there's a catch — you have to spend it all in the PSP™ Value Boutique. Read through the complete list carefully, then allocate dollar amounts to the items that are important to you. You MUST spend all of the $100,000 — no more, no less.

Guidelines:
• Buy only those things most important to YOU — not what others say should be important.
• Pretend you are starting with nothing.
• Do not let parents, priests, friends or teachers influence your purchases.
• Read through the ENTIRE list before purchasing.
items: A process guaranteed to rid the world of prejudice and racism.
An opportunity to help the sick and impoverished and allow them happier lives.
The charisma and resources necessary to become famous (movie star, sports hero, astronaut, author).
The chance to create and implement a sure-fire money-making idea.
A morning massage everyday and breakfast, lunch and dinner cooked by a world class chef.
Absolute enlightenment and understanding of myself.
Consistency throughout my life between thought, word and deed.
The opportunity to be my own boss.
As much money as Bill Gates (even more).
To be President of The World.
The perfect relationship.
My dream house with a breath-taking view, filled with my favorite things.
To be one of the most attractive people in the world.
To be perfectly healthy.
Peace of mind.
My own library filled with every publication I ever wanted to read.
Absolute union with the Almighty in thought and deed.
A world of equal justice and opportunity for everyone.
Enough money to fund an organization that truly helps everyone.
To win the Nobel Prize in my chosen field, and be praised everywhere in the world.
To master every skill needed to be the best in my job.
A genie at my beck and call, with unlimited power to satisfy my every wish so I can enjoy life to the fullest.
To be the wisest person ever to live.
A bottle of 'honesty gas' large enough to cure the whole world of lying.
To be completely independent of anyone.
To own a Texas oil well.
To be a role model and guru to people throughout the world.
To perfectly express my love and caring.
A Super Pass that lets me into any concert, play, opera, art show or ballet and to travel anywhere I want by land, sea or air.
A complete make-over: hair, clothes, manicure, plastic surgery, time at a spa, whatever I want in order to look my best.
The perfect body.
Absolute self-esteem and the confidence to think and say what I feel.
To learn anything instantly and be able to recall it at a moment's notice.
Uninterrupted conversations with any spiritual leader(s) I want, past or present.

content_json.prompt:
After completing the Values Shopping Spree, pair the amounts from items 1+18, 2+19, 3+20, etc. to calculate your total for each value. Then rank these 17 values from highest to lowest spending (your top three are your core values).

Value pairings: Justice (1+18), Humanitarianism (2+19), Recognition (3+20), Achievement (4+21), Pleasure (5+22), Wisdom (6+23), Honesty (7+24), Autonomy (8+25), Economy (9+26), Power (10+27), Love (11+28), Aesthetics (12+29), Physical Attractiveness (13+30), Health (14+31), Feeling of Well-Being (15+32), Knowledge (16+33), Spiritual Well-Being (17+34).

items.label: Humanitarianism — Devotion to the promotion of human welfare (Items 2 + 19)
Recognition — Acknowledgment of one's significance or importance (Items 3 + 20)
Wisdom — Understanding of what is true, right or lasting (Items 6 + 23)
Economy — Abundance of material possessions, wealth (Items 9 + 26)
Aesthetics — Appreciation and enjoyment of beauty for beauty's sake (Items 12 + 29)
Physical Attractiveness — Concern for the appearance of one's body (Items 13 + 30)
Health — Concern for the health of one's body (Items 14 + 31)
Feeling of Well-Being — Freedom from anxiety, imbalance; peace of mind (Items 15 + 32)
Knowledge — Seeking of truth and/or information (Items 16 + 33)
Spiritual Well-Being — Communion with, obedience to, and activity on behalf of a Supreme Being (Items 17 + 34)

content_json.prompt: Based on the Values Shopping Spree exercise, list your top three values (those you spent the most money on) and reflect on how each one shows up as a driving force in your life. How do these values influence your career choices, relationships, and daily decisions?
content_json.placeholder:
1. My top value is ___ because...
2. My second value is ___ because...
3. My third value is ___ because...

sections.title: Roles & Their Demands
sections.subtitle: Filter 4 of 5
sections.description: Examine the roles you play in life and the demands they make upon you. Whether or not you are happy with them, your roles are a driving force — consciously or unconsciously impacting every aspect of your daily life.

opening_quote.text: You can do anything, but not everything.

framing.opening_question: If someone made a documentary of your last 30 days, how many different characters would you play in it?
framing.facilitator_says: We all wear multiple hats. The question isn't whether — it's whether each hat is one we still want to wear, or one we just inherited.
framing.why_it_matters: Roles consume your hours. If you don't audit them, they will consume your life. The Life Line and Past Experience Inventory in this section help you see the shape of your time, not just the content of it. That shape is what your goals will have to fit inside.
framing.closing_reflection: Which role on your list deserves more time than it currently gets — and which one secretly deserves less?
framing.bridge_to_next: Roles are demands on your time. Skills are what travel with you through every role. Let's catalogue them.

reading_material.title: Essentialism: The Disciplined Pursuit of Less — Greg McKeown
reading_material.content: McKeown's central move is to treat your roles as decisions, not obligations. A role inherited from family, employer, or habit is still a role you renew every day by showing up. The book offers a useful filter — 'if it isn't a clear yes, it's a clear no' — for the Life Line exercise. Read this before deciding which roles to keep on the list and which to retire.

exercises.title: EXERCISE: My Life Line — Past, Present and Future

content_json.prompt:
Draw a line (in your workbook or mind) that depicts your life as you see it from beginning to end. Your lifeline may be any shape and go in any direction — a road, river, thread, path, or graph line. Place three marks indicating your birth, your death, and where you are now.

Once you have reflected on your lifeline, write your observations below:

THE PAST (your unique experiences, acquired skills, and personal history):
• The past has happened; we cannot change it.
• Your past has a place in your current life — accept it, reconstruct it, evaluate it creatively.
• The past can yield insight into your unfulfilled potential.

THE PRESENT (the here and now with all its joys, frustrations, priorities and demands):
• You can choose where you want to go based on what satisfies you now.
• You have undeveloped potential that suggests directions for future development.

THE FUTURE (your aspirations, dreams, goals, hopes and fears):
• To a large degree, you create your own future.
• All three perspectives are necessary to develop a more fulfilling life plan.

What themes do you notice across your past, present, and future?
content_json.placeholder:
Looking at my lifeline, I notice...

In my PAST, the most significant experiences were...

In my PRESENT, I am...

For my FUTURE, I hope/fear...

content_json.prompt: Write 10 separate short statements that answer the question 'Who am I?' Then rank these statements according to their importance to YOU — not how you think others would rank them. Ensure that at least 3–4 of your roles are defined in view of your responsibilities or accountabilities to specific individuals or groups (family, children, spouses, etc.).
headers: Who Am I? (complete the sentence: I am...)

content_json.prompt:
Answer each of the following 14 questions as honestly and fully as possible. This inventory helps you identify the experiences, influences, and patterns that have shaped who you are today.

1. Who have been the most influential people in your life and in what way?
2. What were the critical incidents (events) that made you who you are?
3. What have been the major interests in your life so far?
4. What were the significant experiences in your school or college career?
5. What were your significant work experiences?
6. What were the most significant decisions in your life?
7. What makes you alive and excited? What events or activities spur you on?
8. What am I really good at? What strengths do I have to build on?
9. What do I do poorly? What do I need to develop or correct?
10. What do I want to stop doing that affects my effectiveness?
11. What do I want to change in my life?
12. What do I want to start doing much better?
13. What do I want to learn or develop in myself?
14. Who are the persons or organizations that can help me achieve my goals?
content_json.placeholder:
1. The most influential people in my life have been...

2. Critical events that shaped me...

3. My major interests...

exercises.title: EXERCISE: A Contract With Myself
exercises.attribution: (From Professor Muriel James, author of Born to Win, Breaking Free, Passion for Life — on transactional analysis)

content_json.prompt:
Complete this personal contract for change (as suggested by Professor Muriel James, author of Born to Win):

Article 1: What do I want to change in my life for the better?
Article 2: What do I need to get what I want?
Article 3: What am I willing to do?
Article 4: How will I measure my success?
Article 5: How could I sabotage myself from achieving success?
Article 6: What are my next positive steps toward change?
content_json.placeholder:
Article 1 — What I want to change:

Article 2 — What I need:

Article 3 — What I am willing to do:

Article 4 — How I will measure success:

Article 5 — How I could sabotage myself:

Article 6 — My next positive steps:

content_json.prompt:
A Mission/Vision Statement gives you a powerful tool to help you focus and prioritize your life. Write your personal mission statement, addressing four dimensions:

1. My Vision/Mission/Purpose for this life is...
2. In relation to MYSELF...
3. In relation to OTHERS...
4. In relation to THE WORLD...
5. If I put it in one sentence, my mission is...

Tip: Look at your own eyes in the mirror and repeat your mission statement to yourself. Ask: Does it sound right for me? Does it fit me completely as the person I am? Is it MY statement or someone else's?
content_json.placeholder:
My Vision/Mission/Purpose for this life is...

In relation to MYSELF:

In relation to OTHERS:

In relation to THE WORLD:

In one sentence, my mission is:

sections.title: Transferable Skills
sections.subtitle: Filter 5 of 5
sections.description: Identify your gifts, talents, educational history, work experience, hobbies, interests, and passions. Never underestimate your passions in planning your career and life — they are your most powerful transferable assets.

opening_quote.text: Your work is going to fill a large part of your life, and the only way to be truly satisfied is to do what you believe is great work.

framing.opening_question: What is something you do well that other people seem to find difficult?
framing.facilitator_says: Skills aren't just job descriptions. They are patterns — and patterns travel with you wherever you go.
framing.why_it_matters: Knowing your transferable skills means you are never trapped in one role. They are leverage. They are the answer to 'what could I do if I had to start over tomorrow?' Strategy needs options, and your skills inventory is your option set.
framing.closing_reflection: Which skill, if developed, would unlock the most doors for you next year?
framing.bridge_to_next: You have your style, lenses, compass, hats, and toolkit. Now we put it all together — into goals.

reading_material.title: What Color Is Your Parachute? — Richard N. Bolles
reading_material.content: Bolles invented the modern transferable-skills inventory. The book's core exercise — the Flower — separates a skill from the job that taught you the skill, so you can carry it into completely different work. Read the chapter on transferable skills before the inventory exercise; it will help you list skills as verbs (analyse, listen, organise) rather than as job titles.

exercises.title: EXERCISE: Transferable Skills — Dealing with Individuals

content_json.prompt: Rate each skill on a scale of 1 (lowest) to 5 (highest). These are skills you use when dealing with individuals one-on-one.
skills: Take instructions, Serve or Help
Diagnose, Treat or Heal
Do well in conversation, in person, or on the phone
Refer people, or help two to link up
Communicate well in writing (e.g. excellent letters)
Assess, Evaluate, Screen, or Select Individuals
Instruct, Teach, Tutor, or Train Individuals
Persuade, Motivate, Recruit, Sell to Individuals
Advise, Coach, Counsel, Mentor
Represent Others, Interpret Others' Ideas

content_json.prompt: Rate each skill on a scale of 1 (lowest) to 5 (highest). These are skills you use when gathering, managing, storing and retrieving information, data, and ideas.

sections.title: Setting Goals
sections.subtitle: Your Personal Strategic Plan™
sections.description: Now that you have completed all five filters, it's time to set your goals. Research shows that people with a comprehensive set of life goals — not just career goals — are more likely to achieve them and find true satisfaction when they do.

opening_quote.text: A goal without a plan is just a wish.

framing.opening_question: If your future self — five years from now — could send you one sentence, what would it be?
framing.facilitator_says: Now we put it together. Personality + Attitudes + Values + Roles + Skills = the lens through which you set goals that are actually yours, not someone else's.
framing.why_it_matters: This is where Personal Strategic Planning earns its name. Everything before this was preparation. The Cross-Impact Matrix and Goal Achievement Plan exercises in this section are your blueprint for the next chapter — written by you, in your handwriting, using the self-knowledge you just built.
framing.closing_reflection: Which goal on your list scares you the most? That one is probably the most important.

reading_material.title: Goals! — Brian Tracy
reading_material.content: Tracy's framework is procedural: pick the goal, write it down, set a deadline, list everything needed, sort that list into a plan, take action on the plan today, and do something every day toward the most important goal. The Goal Achievement Plan in this section follows the same shape. Tracy's contribution to the workshop is the discipline of writing — until a goal is on paper with a date, it remains a wish (per Saint-Exupéry's opening quote).

exercises.title: EXERCISE: My Life Goal Inventory
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with kind permission from Dr. David Kolb. From Organizational Behavior: An Experiential Approach, Fifth Edition — David Kolb, Irwin M. Rubin and Joyce Osland. Prentice Hall, Englewood Cliffs, N.Y. 07632.)

content_json.prompt:
Describe your aims and goals for ALL areas of your life as fully as possible. Be as specific as you can — include the date by which you wish to have each goal achieved. Sift each goal through your four filters: Personal Style, Attitudes and Values, Roles and their Demands, and Transferable Skills.

Goal Importance: H (High) = very important compared to others | M (Moderate) = moderately important | L (Low) = other goals are more important

Ease of Attainment: H (High) = easily reachable | M (Moderate) = reachable with moderate difficulty | L (Low) = very difficult to reach

Categories:
A — Career Satisfaction: your goals for your future job or career, including specific positions you want to hold.
B — Status and Respect: how important is receiving respect from others? From whom? What groups do you belong to?
C — Personal Relationships: goals in your relationships with colleagues, parents, friends, people in general.
D — Learning and Education: what would you like to know more about? What skills do you want to develop?
E — Material Rewards and Possessions: what kind of wealth is important to you? What possessions would you want?
F — Leisure Satisfaction: goals for leisure time — hobbies, sports, vacations, interests you want to develop.
G — Spiritual Growth and Religion: goals for peace of mind, your search for meaning — relationship to nature, community, devotional life, meditation.
H — Open: goals that don't fit the above categories.

From all the goals in your Life Goal Inventory, select the ones that seem most important to you at this time. Do not choose more than ten (10). List them below in order of preference — most important first, least important last.

One of the major blocks to goal accomplishment is the conflict between goals. Take your top 8 goals and enter them across the top and down the left-hand side. Then use the following symbols to rank the effect each goal has on the others:

+ = helpful impact (working on Goal 1 will HELP me with Goal 3)
- = hindering impact (working on Goal 2 will make it MORE DIFFICULT to accomplish Goal 5)
0 = no impact of any kind

Note: Where a goal meets itself, mark 0 (obviously Goal 1 has no impact where it meets Goal 1).

After completing the matrix, list at least five conflicts in order of importance.

For each of your top 8 goals, work through the following 6 questions AND identify obstacles and action steps. This is your Personal Strategic Plan™.

For each goal, answer:
1. How important is it that you achieve this goal?
2. How does this goal relate to your long-term goals? What conflicts are there?
3. How will you feel when you attain this goal? (Try to imagine yourself with the goal achieved.)
4. How will you feel if you do not attain this goal?
5. What do you think about your chances of succeeding? What will happen if you do succeed?
6. What will happen if you fail?

For removing obstacles, consider:
• Personal shortcomings that could prevent you from achieving the goal
• Obstacles in the world that could prevent achievement
• What you can do to eliminate or lessen each obstacle
• Specific action steps that will move you toward the goal

Complete this for each of your top 8 goals:
content_json.placeholder:
GOAL 1: ___

1. Importance:
2. Long-term relation / conflicts:
3. How I'll feel when attained:
4. How I'll feel if not attained:
5. Chances of success:
6. If I fail:

Personal shortcomings:
World obstacles:
Action steps:

---

GOAL 2: ___
...

content_json.prompt: The purpose of alibis is to cover up weaknesses. The energy used in supporting alibis is usually more than enough to correct the weakness. Check any alibis YOU have used — this is about honest self-awareness, not judgment.

exercises.title: My Declaration of Self-Esteem
exercises.attribution: (by Virginia Satir, 1970 — Virginia Satir is known as the grandmother of family therapy)

content_json.prompt:
Read through Virginia Satir's Declaration of Self-Esteem (1970), then write any thoughts or feelings it brings up for you:

"I am me. In all the world, there is no one exactly like me. There are persons who have some parts of me, but no one adds up exactly like me. Therefore, everything that comes out of me is authentically mine because I alone chose it.

I own everything about me — my body, including everything it does; my mind, including all its thoughts and ideas; my eyes, including the images of all they behold; my feelings, whatever they may be — anger, joy, frustration, love, disappointment, excitement; my mouth, and all the words that come out of it; my voice, loud or soft, and all my actions, whether they be to others or to myself.

I own my fantasies, my dreams, my hopes, my fears. I own all my triumphs and successes, all my failures, and mistakes.

Because I own all of me, I can become intimately acquainted with me. By so doing I can love me and be friendly with me in all my parts. I can then make it possible for all of me to work in my best interest.

I know there are aspects about myself that puzzle me, and other aspects I do not know. But as long as I am friendly and loving to myself, I can courageously and hopefully look for solutions to the puzzles and for ways to find out more about me.

I own me, and therefore I can engineer me. I am me and I am okay."

What thoughts or feelings arise in response to this declaration? What parts resonate most deeply with you?
content_json.placeholder:
Reading the Declaration of Self-Esteem, I feel...

The parts that resonate most with me are...

As I complete my Personal Strategic Plan™, I commit to...
